← The Drifter's Almanac
01 Quick shelters 02 Homes 03 Grand & stone 04 Workshops 05 Furnaces & industry 06 Farming & crops 07 Livestock & animals 08 Power & machines 09 Water & sanitation 10 Defence & fortification 11 Crossings & transport 12 Mining & underground 13 Temples & monuments 14 Civic & social 15 Decorative & garden 16 Towers & verticals 17 Coastal & naval 18 Storage & logistics 19 Advanced contraptions 20 Wilderness & utility

316 stylised 3D plans, not block lists.
For decorative technique see The Architect;
for course-by-course working
plans see The Homestead. v1.22.3.

Vintage Story · v1.22.3 · Building gallery

The Pattern Book

A catalogue of things to build, drawn in 3D so you can see the shape before you place a block. 316 structures across 20 themes, from a night-one lean-to to powered mill complexes. Each card gives the footprint, the key materials and what it's best for. Pick one, copy the massing, then dress the detail with The Architect.

316 structures 3D isometric 20 themes 🧱 Massing, not block lists

01 — Night one · 14 builds

Quick shelters

Get a roof over your head fast. None are pretty, but each survives a night while you scout for somewhere permanent.

Lean-to shelter

Day oneTemporary
Footprint6×4
Key materialsLogs, sticks, dry grass
Best forA roof over your head on night one

A back wall, two posts, one sloped roof. Buys a dry night while you plan the real base.

Earth dugout

CheapWarm
Footprint6×4
Key materialsDug soil, a door, a chimney
Best forA warm hole when wood is scarce

Cut into a bank and frame a door. Soil insulates, so it stays warmer than a timber hut in deep cold.

A-frame

SimpleSheds rain
Footprint4×6
Key materialsLogs, thatch
Best forA steep roof that needs no walls

The roof is the building. Steep sides shed rain and snow; the gable ends hold a door and a vent.

Hide tent

NomadPack up
Footprint4×4 round
Key materialsPoles, hides
Best forA movable camp while exploring

A cone of hides on poles. Light, quick, and you can take it with you when you move on.

Roundhouse

CozyThatch cone
Footprint5×5 round
Key materialsCob/plaster, thatch
Best forA snug single room

Round cob walls under a conical thatch roof. No corners to draught, cheap to raise.

Windbreak camp

First minutesBedroll
Footprint5×3
Key materialsA wall, a fire, a bedroll
Best forStopping for the night on the move

A low wall to block the wind, a fire ring and a bedroll. The absolute minimum that counts as 'camp'.

Tarp & bedroll

One minuteBedroll
Footprint4×3
Key materialsTwo poles, a tarp, a bedroll
Best forThe fastest possible night stop

A cloth slung between two poles over a bedroll. No walls, just a roof to keep the dew off.

Brush hut

Debris shelterNo tools
Footprint4×4 round
Key materialsSticks, leaves
Best forSurviving with nothing but the forest

A low cone of leaned sticks piled with leaf litter. Ugly, but it traps body heat through a night.

Snow quinzhee

Snow domeWinter
Footprint5×5 dome
Key materialsPacked snow
Best forShelter on a frozen, treeless slope

A dome of packed snow hollowed out inside. Snow insulates well; cut a small low door and a vent.

Tripod tarp

Three polesConical
Footprint4×4 round
Key materialsThree poles, a tarp
Best forA free-standing shelter, no wall needed

Three lashed poles draped with cloth make a stable cone. Stands anywhere; needs no tree or bank.

Two-post tarp lean-to

Open frontTarp
Footprint5×4
Key materialsFour poles, a tarp
Best forA roomy open shelter facing a fire

A tarp pitched as a slope on a four-post frame, no back wall. Open the low side to a fire.

Log windbreak ring

Stacked logsFire ring
Footprint6×6 round
Key materialsLogs, a fire
Best forBlocking wind from every side

A near-circle of stacked logs around a central fire. Leave one gap to step in out of the gale.

Fire pit & log seats

Gather roundSeating
Footprint6×6
Key materialsStone ring, log benches
Best forA social camp hearth

A stone fire ring with split-log seats around it. The heart of a camp once you stop moving.

Raised sleeping platform

Off the groundDry
Footprint4×4 raised
Key materialsPoles, planks, a tarp
Best forSleeping above damp or bugs

A plank deck on short posts with a tarp over. Keeps you off wet, crawling ground in the warm season.

02 — Settling in · 18 builds

Homes

The builds you actually live in for a season or three: cabins, cottages, longhouses, timber-frame and houses over the water.

Log cabin

Starter homeGable roof
Footprint7×6
Key materialsLogs, planks, thatch
Best forYour first permanent base

Solid log walls and a pitched gable roof. Smooth the slope with stairs and slabs in-game.

Hip-roof house

CottageHip roof
Footprint7×7
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forA tidy weatherproof cottage

A four-sided hip roof sheds rain on every side and overhangs the walls.

Cob cottage

CozyBig hearth
Footprint5×5
Key materialsCob, timber, thatch
Best forA warm one-room home

Thick cob walls hold heat; an oversized chimney runs a hearth that warms the whole room.

Longhouse

CommunalLong span
Footprint11×5
Key materialsLogs, thatch
Best forLots of space, one roofline

A long footprint under one ridge. Cheap to roof per square, easy to extend lengthwise.

Stilt house

Over waterDry
Footprint5×4 raised
Key materialsPosts, logs, thatch
Best forLiving on a lake or marsh edge

Raised on posts above the waterline. A ladder is the way up; the deck stays dry when the level rises.

Townhouse

TallSmall footprint
Footprint4×4 × 8
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forGoing up where land is tight

Three storeys on a small footprint. Stack the windows and run a chimney up the gable end.

Timber-frame house

Exposed beamsPlaster infill
Footprint7×6
Key materialsOak frame, plaster, shingles
Best forA handsome two-storey townish home

Dark beams over white plaster. Mark out posts, sills, rails and braces, then fill the panels.

Stone cottage

Rubble wallsSnug
Footprint6×5
Key materialsFieldstone, timber, slate
Best forA sturdy cold-climate one-room home

Low stone walls under a steep slate gable. Heavy build, but it holds heat and shrugs off weather.

Great thatched cottage

Deep thatchCosy
Footprint8×6
Key materialsCob, timber, thatch
Best forA roomy storybook cottage

Fat cob walls under a deep, overhanging thatch. Sweep the eaves low for that bundled-reed look.

Dogtrot cabin

Two roomsOpen breezeway
Footprint9×5
Key materialsLogs, planks, shingles
Best forTwo cabins joined by a shaded passage

Two log pens with an open breezeway between, all under one roof. The trot stays cool in summer.

L-shaped house

Two wingsHip roofs
Footprint8×8
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forWrapping rooms around a corner

Two hipped wings meeting at a right angle. The inside of the L makes a sheltered yard or porch.

Covered-porch house

Front porchShed roof
Footprint7×6 + porch
Key materialsTimber, planks, shingles
Best forA shaded seat out front

A row of posts carries a shed roof over the front door. Floor it with planks for a porch to sit on.

Dormer-roof house

Roof windowsAttic
Footprint7×6
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forLighting a lived-in attic floor

Little gabled dormers pop out of the main slope to light the loft. Keep them evenly spaced.

Plank house

Board wallsLow pitch
Footprint8×5
Key materialsSawn planks, shingles
Best forA clean-lined house once you have a saw

Vertical plank cladding with batten lines, under a low gable. Cheap once the sawmill runs.

Courtyard house

U-planInner yard
Footprint9×8
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forWrapping rooms around a private yard

Three ranges form a U around an open court. Wall off the fourth side for a snug, sheltered home.

Saltbox house

Long rear slopeAsymmetric
Footprint7×6
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forTwo storeys front, lean-to rear

Tall two-storey front, single-storey back, joined by one long catslide roof. A classic cold-side profile.

House with lean-to shed

Attached shedExtension
Footprint7×6 + shed
Key materialsLogs, planks, shingles
Best forAdding store space onto a cabin

A gabled cabin with a lower lean-to shed bolted to one end. Cheapest way to grow a small home.

Cross-plan house

CruciformTwin gables
Footprint8×8
Key materialsPlaster, timber, shingles
Best forA grander home with crossing wings

Two gabled ranges crossing at the centre make a plus-shaped plan with four bright gable ends.

03 — Built to last · 15 builds

Grand & stone

When the base is established and stone is cheap: homesteads, manors, halls, keeps and chateaux.

Two-storey homestead

Family baseTwo floors
Footprint7×6 × 2
Key materialsPlaster, timber frame, shingles
Best forA long-term home with room to grow

Two full floors under one gable. Stack windows to light both levels; the chimney serves a ground-floor hearth.

Manor with wings

EstateMulti-wing
Footprint14×6
Key materialsPlaster, stone, shingles
Best forA showpiece estate house

A tall central block flanked by two gabled wings. Symmetry and chimneys do the heavy lifting.

Stone keep

FortifiedTurret
Footprint6×6 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA defensible heart for a walled base

A massive square block with a crenellated top and a taller corner turret. Thick walls, few openings.

Watchtower

LookoutLandmark
Footprint3×3 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forSeeing trouble before it arrives

A tapered shaft with a crenellated top. Height is the point; light it as a beacon you can run home to.

Lighthouse

CoastBeacon
Footprint4×4 round × tall
Key materialsWhitewashed stone, glass
Best forMarking a harbour or home shore

A tapered round tower with a glazed lantern room and a light. Build it on a headland you can see from the sea.

Château

Round towersConical caps
Footprint10×8 + towers
Key materialsWhitestone, slate
Best forA fairy-tale estate centrepiece

A central block with round corner towers under tall conical caps. Symmetry and height sell the fantasy.

Great hall

Long spanHigh gable
Footprint12×6 × tall
Key materialsTimber, stone, shingles
Best forA communal feasting hall

One vast room under a tall, steep gable. Buttress the long walls and let the roof do the drama.

Fortified stone hall

BattlementsStrong
Footprint10×7 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA defensible main hall

A heavy stone hall with a crenellated parapet and a stair turret. Few, high windows; thick walls.

Gabled stone mansion

Two storeysSymmetric
Footprint10×7 × 2
Key materialsWhitestone, slate
Best forA formal two-storey residence

A symmetrical stone block, two floors of ranked windows, twin chimneys and a central door.

U-plan manor

Two wingsCourt of honour
Footprint12×9
Key materialsPlaster, stone, shingles
Best forA grand house framing a forecourt

A central range with two forward wings enclosing a court of honour. Approach is the whole point.

Colonnaded villa

ColumnsPortico
Footprint10×6 + colonnade
Key materialsWhitestone, marble
Best forA classical show frontage

A long single-storey block fronted by a row of columns under a flat entablature. Add steps up.

Gatehouse-manor

Twin towersArch
Footprint10×6 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA manor you enter through a gate

Two square towers flank a tall arched passage with living rooms above. Imposing arrival, real defence.

Three-storey tower house

VerticalCompact
Footprint5×5 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber, slate
Best forA defensible home on a tight plot

A tall square tower of stacked rooms with a battlemented walk. Living up high, store and hall below.

Mansard-roof mansion

Steep roofDormers
Footprint9×7 × 2
Key materialsPlaster, slate
Best forAn elegant double-pitched roofline

A tall block capped by a steep mansard — a near-vertical lower slope, modelled as a slate frustum.

Winged lodge

Hunting lodgeLow wings
Footprint12×6
Key materialsLogs, stone, shingles
Best forA long, low retreat in the woods

A tall central hall between two low wings, all timber and stone. Generous porch, big hearth.

04 — Making things · 19 builds

Workshops

Sheds and halls for the trades — smithing, sawing, tanning, brewing, baking, weaving, glass and carpentry.

Open smithy

ForgeOpen shed
Footprint7×6
Key materialsPosts, shingles, stone
Best forWorking metal without a stuffy room

Four posts, a shed roof, a stone forge and an anvil. Open sides vent the heat and smoke.

Water-powered sawmill

LumberWaterwheel
Footprint5×4 + channel
Key materialsTimber, gears, a saw
Best forCutting logs into planks in bulk

A waterwheel drives a saw inside the mill house. Stack cut logs by the door and feed them through.

Tannery

LeatherDrying racks
Footprint7×5
Key materialsPosts, rails, a vat
Best forCuring hides into leather

An open shed of drying racks with a soaking vat. Hides hang to dry once they're out of the tan.

Brewery

DrinkBarrels
Footprint5×5
Key materialsPlaster, timber, barrels
Best forFermenting cider, mead and ale

A warm room with a chimney and a yard of barrels. Keep the casks shaded and cool.

Bakery & oven

BreadClay oven
Footprint4×4 + oven
Key materialsPlaster, stone, clay
Best forBaking bread and pies

A small shelter beside a domed clay oven with a stone flue. Fire it down to embers, then bake.

Weaver's hall

ClothLoom
Footprint6×5
Key materialsLogs, thatch
Best forSpinning and weaving fabric

A lit hall with a standing loom by the window. Good light is the whole job, so keep one side glazed.

Stable

AnimalsOpen front
Footprint8×5
Key materialsLogs, thatch
Best forHousing horses or oxen

A back wall, an open front and stall dividers. Pile hay at one end and muck out the other.

Carpenter's shop

WoodworkSawhorses
Footprint7×6
Key materialsLogs, planks, shingles
Best forCutting joints and building furniture

An open-fronted shed with sawhorses and stacked lumber. Keep the offcuts; they feed the firebox.

Mason's yard

StoneCrane
Footprint8×7
Key materialsStone blocks, timber crane
Best forCutting and dressing building stone

Squared blocks stacked by a timber jib crane. Dress stone here before it goes to the wall.

Glassworks

GlassTall chimney
Footprint6×5 + chimney
Key materialsBrick, stone, glass
Best forBlowing glass from sand and ash

A hot furnace house under a tall draught chimney. Keep the fire roaring; glass needs serious heat.

Pottery works

ClayWheel & kiln
Footprint8×6
Key materialsPlaster, clay, timber
Best forThrowing pots and firing them

A throwing wheel under cover, drying racks of greenware, and a kiln to fire it all.

Ropewalk

RopeLong shed
Footprint13×3
Key materialsPosts, planks, thatch
Best forTwisting long lengths of rope

A very long, low building so fibre can be spun into rope without a single join.

Sawpit

LumberPit saw
Footprint6×4 pit
Key materialsDug pit, a log, frame
Best forRipping logs into boards by hand

A log over a dug pit; one sawyer above, one below. Slow, but needs no water or wind.

Charcoal-burner's hut

FuelForest camp
Footprint5×5
Key materialsPoles, turf, a wood stack
Best forTending a slow charcoal burn

A turf-roofed hut beside a stacked wood cord. The burner sleeps here to watch the smoulder.

Walled smithy

ForgeChimney
Footprint6×5 + chimney
Key materialsStone, timber, slate
Best forHeavy forging out of the weather

A closed stone forge with a big flue chimney. Walls keep the cold off the work in deep winter.

Malthouse

MaltKiln cowl
Footprint6×5 + cowl
Key materialsPlaster, timber, a cowl
Best forMalting grain for brewing

A drying floor under a pyramid kiln topped with a turning cowl that vents the smoke downwind.

Dyer's yard

DyeDrying cloth
Footprint7×6
Key materialsStone vats, posts, cloth
Best forDyeing wool and hanging it to dry

A row of dye vats with lengths of bright cloth on the line. The colours mark a busy dyer.

Chandler's shop

CandlesTallow
Footprint5×5
Key materialsPlaster, timber, a vat
Best forDipping candles from tallow or wax

A small shop with a melting vat and dipping frames hung with candles. Light and a steady hand.

Printshop & scriptorium

BooksPress
Footprint6×5
Key materialsTimber frame, plaster
Best forSetting type and printing pages

A well-lit hall with a screw press and drying lines of fresh sheets. Tall windows for close work.

05 — Fire & ore · 16 builds

Furnaces & industry

The hot end of the tech tree: charcoal, kilns, bloomeries, blast and cementation furnaces, foundries and quarries.

Charcoal pit

FuelSmoulder
Footprint5×5 mound
Key materialsFirewood, soil
Best forTurning stacked wood into charcoal

A cone of firewood buried under soil so it smoulders, not burns. Charcoal feeds every furnace past the forge.

Pottery kiln

FiringPottery
Footprint4×4 round
Key materialsClay bricks
Best forHard-firing pots, crucibles and molds

A round clay chamber with a domed top and a stoke hole. Fire your formed clay here before it holds water or metal.

Bloomery stack

IronSmelt
Footprint2×2 stack
Key materialsFire clay, bellows
Best forSmelting iron ore into a bloom

A tall, narrow clay chimney. Layer ore and charcoal, work the bellows, tap a spongy bloom from the base.

Blast furnace

IronScaffold
Footprint3×3 stack + frame
Key materialsStone, timber, bellows
Best forSmelting iron in bigger batches

A tall stone stack charged from a timber platform. A tap arch at the base lets the metal out.

Cementation furnace

SteelChimney
Footprint5×4 + flue
Key materialsStone, fire clay
Best forCarburising iron into blister steel

A sealed furnace house under a tall flue. It runs for days, soaking iron bars in charcoal to make steel.

Clay quarry

ClayExcavation
Footprint8×6 pit
Key materialsDug clay, a ladder
Best forDigging clay, gravel and stone in bulk

A stepped pit worked down in benches with a ladder and a barrow. Clay is the base of pottery and cob.

Beehive kiln

FiringDome
Footprint5×5 dome
Key materialsBrick
Best forBatch-firing brick and pottery

A round brick dome stoked through low arches. The shape holds heat evenly across a big load.

Updraft kiln

FiringStack
Footprint4×4 + vents
Key materialsFire brick, clay
Best forFiring pottery with a rising draught

A squat brick chamber with a firebox below and vents up top. Heat rises straight through the ware.

Lime kiln

LimeTall stone
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone, fire clay
Best forBurning limestone into quicklime

A tall stone shaft built into a bank, charged from the top and drawn from an arch at the foot.

Brick clamp

BrickStacked
Footprint6×4 stack
Key materialsGreen bricks, fuel
Best forFiring bricks without a permanent kiln

Raw bricks stacked with fuel and capped with mud, then lit. Cheap, but uneven across the pile.

Crucible furnace

SteelCompact
Footprint3×3 + bellows
Key materialsFire brick, clay
Best forMelting metal in sealed crucibles

A small, deep brick furnace fed by bellows. Seal the crucible and bring it to a white heat.

Reverberatory furnace

SmeltLong flue
Footprint8×4 + chimney
Key materialsBrick, stone, slate
Best forSmelting without fuel touching ore

A long low brick furnace that bounces flame off the roof onto the charge, with a tall draught stack.

Smeltery shed

OreOpen furnace
Footprint7×6
Key materialsPosts, stone, shingles
Best forRunning a furnace under open cover

An open shed over a stone furnace and a slag heap. Open sides let the choking fumes clear.

Twin-stack foundry

CastingTwo stacks
Footprint7×6 + stacks
Key materialsBrick, stone, slate
Best forCasting metal in volume

A casting hall flanked by two tall stacks. Pour from the furnaces into moulds on the floor.

Tar kiln

PitchEarth mound
Footprint5×5 mound
Key materialsResin wood, soil
Best forCooking pitch and tar from pinewood

A buried mound of resinous wood with a sloped floor that runs hot tar out to a barrel.

Potash furnace

AshLeaching
Footprint6×5
Key materialsStone, iron pans
Best forBoiling wood ash down to potash

A stone hearth under iron pans where leached ash is boiled to potash for soap and glass.

06 — Food & crops · 16 builds

Farming & crops

Feed the base: tilled fields, terraces, orchards, vineyards, greenhouses and the stores that hold the harvest.

Tilled field

CropsFenced
Footprint8×6 plot
Key materialsTilled soil, fencing
Best forGrowing grain and vegetables

Furrowed rows inside a fence, with a scarecrow. Rotate crops by nutrient so the soil keeps giving.

Orchard

FruitTrees
Footprint10×7
Key materialsSaplings, fencing
Best forLong-term fruit and nut supply

Fruit trees in rows behind a fence. Slow to start, but a mature orchard feeds you every autumn.

Greenhouse

Winter cropsGlass
Footprint7×5
Key materialsStone sill, glass panes
Best forGrowing crops through a hard winter

Glass walls and roof over a low stone sill. Traps warmth so tender crops keep going when the ground freezes.

Granary on stilts

StorageDry
Footprint5×5 raised
Key materialsLogs, thatch
Best forKeeping grain dry and away from vermin

Raised on posts so damp and animals can't reach the grain. A ladder is the only way up.

Terraced fields

HillsideCrops
Footprint10×7 stepped
Key materialsStone risers, tilled soil
Best forFarming a slope without erosion

Step the hillside into level beds held by stone risers. Each terrace catches its own water.

Flooded paddy

RiceWater
Footprint9×7 plot
Key materialsBunded soil, water
Best forGrowing rice in standing water

Low earth bunds hold a shallow flood. Green shoots stand in rows above the water.

Vineyard rows

GrapesTrellis
Footprint10×6
Key materialsPosts, wire, vines
Best forGrowing grapes for wine

Low posts strung with wire carry the vines. Keep rows wide enough to walk and pick.

Hop yard

HopsTall poles
Footprint9×6
Key materialsTall poles, twine
Best forClimbing hops for brewing

Hops climb tall poles strung overhead. Train the bines up the twine each spring.

Kitchen garden

Raised bedsPaths
Footprint8×7
Key materialsTimber beds, gravel paths
Best forMixed vegetables by the door

Boxed raised beds split by gravel paths. Keep it close to the kitchen for daily picking.

Windbreak rows

ShelterTrees
Footprint11×6
Key materialsSaplings, fencing
Best forSheltering a field from wind

A line of trees beside the crop breaks the wind and holds the soil. Plant tall species upwind.

Scarecrow field

CropsScarecrow
Footprint9×7 plot
Key materialsTilled soil, a scarecrow
Best forKeeping birds off ripening grain

A big straw scarecrow lords over the rows. Move him about so the birds never settle.

Mushroom shed

FungiDark
Footprint6×5
Key materialsLogs, dark timber
Best forGrowing mushrooms out of the sun

A windowless dark shed packed with inoculated logs. Damp, shaded and cool is the whole trick.

Herb parterre

HerbsFormal
Footprint7×7
Key materialsClipped hedge, gravel
Best forA tidy formal herb garden

Four clipped beds around a central pot, divided by gravel walks. As pretty as it is useful.

Threshing floor

HarvestPaved
Footprint7×7 round
Key materialsPaved stone, a cart
Best forThreshing and winnowing grain

A round paved floor for beating grain from the straw. A flail leans by the loaded cart.

Pumpkin patch

SquashSprawling
Footprint8×6 plot
Key materialsTilled soil, vines
Best forGrowing pumpkins and squash

Sprawling vines on low mounds with fat orange fruit. Give them room; they ramble everywhere.

Polytunnel

Glass archCrops
Footprint8×4 arch
Key materialsHoops, glass/film
Best forCheap covered growing space

A low glazed arch over rows of beds. Cheaper than a greenhouse, just as warm in spring.

07 — Beasts · 18 builds

Livestock & animals

Housing for the herd and flock: barns, stables, coops, sties, dovecotes, hives, folds and ponds.

Barn & paddock

LivestockStorage
Footprint7×6 + pen
Key materialsLogs, thatch, fencing
Best forHousing animals and feed

A big door for hauling, a steep roof for hay and a fenced paddock alongside for grazing.

Chicken coop

PoultryRaised
Footprint3×3 + run
Key materialsPlanks, wire/fence
Best forEggs and meat from hens

A raised hutch with a ramp and a fenced run. Off the ground keeps it dry and predator-shy.

Pigsty

PigsMud
Footprint6×5 pen
Key materialsRails, a shelter
Best forRaising pigs for meat and fat

A low-railed pen with a wallow and a corner shelter. Pigs root everything, so fence it well.

Dovecote

BirdsTower
Footprint3×3 tower
Key materialsPlaster, timber
Best forPigeons for eggs and meat

A little tower full of nesting holes under a conical cap. Birds come and go as they please.

Apiary

BeesHoney
Footprint5×3
Key materialsSkeps, a bench
Best forHoney and wax from hives

A row of skeps on a bench near flowers. Honey sweetens food; wax seals and waterproofs.

Haystack

FeedSimple
Footprint4×4 mound
Key materialsDried grass
Best forStoring winter feed outdoors

A thatched cone of dried grass. Cheap winter feed; cap it well so the rain runs off.

Open cattle barn

CattleOpen-sided
Footprint9×6
Key materialsPosts, beams, shingles
Best forSheltering cattle that need air

A big roof on posts, open on three sides. Cattle shelter from sun and rain but stay well aired.

Sheepfold

SheepRound pen
Footprint8×7 oval
Key materialsRails, a shelter
Best forPenning a flock with a shelter

A round railed fold with a corner shelter against the weather. Easy to herd into and count.

Horse paddock

HorsesField shelter
Footprint10×7 + shelter
Key materialsRail fence, a shelter
Best forTurning horses out with cover

A wide railed paddock with an open field shelter in one corner. Room to roam, cover when needed.

Boar pen & sty

PigsLarge
Footprint9×6 pen
Key materialsHeavy rails, a sty
Best forRaising a large herd of pigs

A heavy double-railed run with a wallow and a long sty. Build it stout; big pigs push hard.

Free-range run

PoultryHenhouse
Footprint9×6 run
Key materialsMesh fence, a hen-house
Best forFree-ranging hens behind mesh

A roomy fenced run with a small raised hen-house and a ramp. Lets hens scratch but keeps foxes out.

Rabbit hutch

RabbitsOn legs
Footprint4×2 raised
Key materialsPlanks, wire mesh
Best forKeeping rabbits off the ground

A two-bay hutch up on legs with a wire run and a sleeping box. Raised keeps it dry and safe.

Duck pond

DucksRamp
Footprint8×6 pond
Key materialsWater, a ramp, a house
Best forDucks with water to paddle

A little pond with a planked ramp up to a small house on the bank. Ducks need water to thrive.

Goat shed & rock

GoatsClimbing
Footprint8×6 pen
Key materialsRails, a shed, a rock
Best forGoats that need to climb

A railed pen with a low shed and a stack of boulders to clamber. Goats climb anything, so give them this.

Box-hive yard

BeesBox hives
Footprint7×4
Key materialsBox hives, a fence
Best forBoxed bee colonies near flowers

A fenced row of stacked box hives on stands. Frame hives give more honey than skeps and are easy to inspect.

Round dovecote

PigeonsBig tower
Footprint5×5 round tower
Key materialsStone, timber, tiles
Best forA large flock of pigeons

A broad round tower riddled with nest holes under a tiled dome and a lantern for the birds to enter.

Carp pond & jetty

FishJetty
Footprint9×7 pond
Key materialsWater, posts, planks
Best forFarming fish in a still pond

A stocked pond with a short jetty to feed and net from. Still, shaded water keeps the carp fat.

Milking parlour

DairyCows
Footprint7×5 shed
Key materialsPlaster, timber, tiles
Best forMilking cows under cover

A clean walled shed with stalls and a wide door for the herd. Cool, washable and out of the muck.

08 — Mechanical power · 29 builds

Power & machines

Turn wind, water and gears into work: windmills, waterwheels, helve hammers, gear towers and powered mills.

Windmill

PowerSails
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone, cloth sails, gears
Best forFree rotation to grind and hammer

A tapered tower with four sail blades. Put it high and in the open; wind strength climbs with altitude.

Waterwheel mill

PowerFlowing water
Footprint5×4 + channel
Key materialsTimber, gears
Best forSteady power day and night from a stream

A vertical wheel in flowing water drives the mill beside it. Unlike wind, the current never drops.

Helve-hammer shed

ForgingAutomation
Footprint8×5
Key materialsPosts, a beam, an anvil
Best forMechanically hammering metal

A powered beam lifts and drops a hammer onto an anvil. Pair it with a windmill or waterwheel axle.

Hand-crank quern

GrindManual
Footprint3×3
Key materialsStone, a wood handle
Best forGrinding flour by hand before any power

Two stone discs on a bench, turned by a side crank. The first way to mill grain; slow but needs nothing else.

Treadwheel

Manual powerWheel
Footprint4×4
Key materialsTimber, an axle
Best forFoot power to drive a small machine

A person walks inside the big wheel to turn an axle. Slow but reliable where there is no wind or stream.

Ox-driven mill

Animal powerSweep
Footprint7×7 round
Key materialsTimber sweep, a millstone
Best forAnimal power when wind and water are absent

An ox walks a circle pushing a sweep beam that turns a central millstone. A roof keeps the works dry.

Post windmill

WindSmall
Footprint4×4 on a post
Key materialsTimber, cloth sails
Best forAn early windmill you can turn to the wind

The whole timber body pivots on one great post to face the wind. Small, all-wood and cheap to raise.

Tower windmill

WindBig
Footprint5×5 round × tall
Key materialsStone, a cap, cloth sails
Best forA large stone mill where only the cap turns

A fixed stone tower with a rotating timber cap and four big sails. More power and storage than a post mill.

Undershot wheel

WaterFlat stream
Footprint5×4 + channel
Key materialsTimber, a paddle wheel
Best forPower from a slow, flat stream

The current pushes the bottom paddles. Works on flat water with no drop, but gives less power than overshot.

Overshot wheel

WaterLaunder
Footprint5×4 + flume
Key materialsTimber, a buckets wheel
Best forMaximum power where water can drop in from above

Water poured over the top fills buckets and turns the wheel by weight. The strongest small water drive.

Gear & axle tower

TransmissionGears
Footprint3×3 × tall
Key materialsTimber, gearwheels
Best forCarrying rotation up or across to machines

A frame of axles and gearwheels that relays power from a wheel to wherever the work is. The mill's spine.

Helve-hammer mill

ForgingPowered
Footprint8×5
Key materialsTimber, a cam shaft, an anvil
Best forDrop-hammering metal off an axle

A cam on the drive shaft lifts a heavy beam that falls onto the anvil. Walled in to keep the works dry.

Pulverizer shed

CrushingPowered
Footprint6×5
Key materialsPosts, a stamp, a mortar
Best forCrushing ore or bone into powder

A cam lifts a heavy stamp that drops into a stone mortar. An open shed vents the dust.

Grindstone shed

SharpeningPowered
Footprint5×4
Key materialsPosts, a wheelstone, a trough
Best forPower-sharpening tools and blades

A spinning sandstone wheel runs in a water trough to keep an edge cool. Belt it to the mill axle.

Crank-bellows forge

ForgeManual
Footprint5×4
Key materialsStone, a bellows, a crank
Best forForcing air into a forge by hand crank

A great bellows feeds the forge fire, worked by a hand crank instead of a foot. Hotter fire, faster work.

Tide mill

TidalWater
Footprint9×7 + pond
Key materialsStone mill, a tide pond, a wheel
Best forMilling from a trapped tide on the ebb

A gate traps the rising tide in a pond; on the ebb the water runs out under a wheel. Works twice a day with the tides.

Windpump

WindDrainage
Footprint5×5 × tall
Key materialsTimber tower, a fan, a scoop wheel
Best forLifting water off low land by wind

A wind fan turns a scoop wheel that lifts water up out of a drain. The classic land-drainage windmill.

Horse gin

Animal powerWhim
Footprint8×8 round
Key materialsTimber drum, a sweep, a drumhead
Best forAnimal-hauling loads up a shaft

A horse walks a circle to turn a big horizontal drum that winds a rope up a shaft. A pithead winding gin.

Aerial ropeway

TransportCable
Footprint13 span × tall
Key materialsTimber trestles, a cable, buckets
Best forCarrying ore over rough ground on a cable

Loaded buckets ride a slung cable between trestle towers. Spans gullies that no cart road could cross.

Water funicular

InclineCounterbalance
Footprint10×6 incline
Key materialsStone ramp, rails, two cars
Best forA water-ballast cliff railway

Two cars on an inclined track balance on a cable; filling the top car's tank with water hauls the other up.

Fulling mill

ClothPowered
Footprint8×5 + wheel
Key materialsTimber hall, fulling stocks, a wheel
Best forPounding woollen cloth in water

Cammed wooden stocks beat soaked cloth in troughs to thicken it. The wheel drives the heavy hammers all day.

Paper mill

PulpPowered
Footprint8×6 + wheel
Key materialsTimber hall, stampers, drying loft
Best forBeating rag to pulp by waterwheel

Cammed stampers pound rag into pulp; an airy slatted loft above dries the finished sheets. Wheel-driven throughout.

Edge-runner oil press

PressAnimal power
Footprint6×6 round
Key materialsStone bed, a runner stone, a beam
Best forCrushing seed or olives for oil

A heavy stone wheel rolls upright around a stone bed to crush seed; a screw beam then presses out the oil.

Screw cider press

PressManual
Footprint5×5
Key materialsOak frame, a great screw, a trough
Best forPressing pomace for juice

A turned great screw squeezes a stacked cheese of pomace; juice runs to a trough below. Cranked down by a bar.

Rolling mill

IronPowered
Footprint9×5 + wheel
Key materialsTimber hall, iron rolls, a wheel
Best forSqueezing hot iron into bar and plate

Geared iron rolls driven off the wheel squeeze hot blooms into flat bar. A glowing slot shows the work passing through.

Wire-drawing shed

WirePowered
Footprint7×5
Key materialsPosts, draw-plates, capstans
Best forPulling metal rod down to wire

Water-driven capstans haul rod through draw-plates to thin it to wire, then wind it on reels. An open working shed.

Powder mill

HazardPowered
Footprint8×6
Key materialsLight walls, edge runners, a blast wall
Best forMilling gunpowder behind a blast bank

Edge-runner stones grind the charge under a flimsy roof set to blow off; a stout earth bank shields the rest of the works.

Double-wheel mill

WaterTwin wheels
Footprint9×6 + wheels
Key materialsStone mill, two waterwheels
Best forTwice the grind off two side-by-side wheels

Two waterwheels on one race drive two sets of stones in a single mill. Double the work for one head of water.

Gear bridge

TransmissionSpan
Footprint11 span
Key materialsTimber trestles, a line shaft, gears
Best forCarrying drive across a stream to a mill

A raised line shaft on trestles relays rotation from a wheel across a gap, geared down at the far end to the work.

09 — Water & sanitation · 23 builds

Water & sanitation

Catch, lift, carry and clean water: wells, towers, cisterns, fountains, aqueducts, baths and drains.

Covered well

WaterUtility
Footprint3×3
Key materialsStone, timber, rope
Best forClean water at the doorstep

A stone ring, two posts and a little gable to keep rain out. Dig to the water table first.

Water tower

StoragePressure
Footprint3×3 × tall
Key materialsTimber frame, a tank
Best forGravity-fed water across a base

A tank up on a timber frame. Height gives pressure, so it can feed troughs and fountains downhill.

Cistern & pump

WaterReserve
Footprint5×5 tank
Key materialsStone, a pump
Best forCatching and storing rainwater

A stone tank that holds rain, with a pump to draw it. A dry-season reserve that needs no stream.

Windlass well

WaterRope
Footprint3×3
Key materialsStone, a roller, rope
Best forLifting a heavy bucket by crank rope

A rope winds on a barrel turned by a crank to haul the bucket up. Easier than a bare rope for deep wells.

Stone water tower

StoragePressure
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone base, a timber tank
Best forA large gravity reservoir for a town

A big tank on a stone tower gives strong, steady pressure downhill. Bigger sibling of the timber water tower.

Stone cistern

ReserveLidded
Footprint6×6 tank
Key materialsStone, a timber lid
Best forA large covered rainwater reserve

A big stone tank with a partial timber lid to cut evaporation. Feed it from roof gutters; draw from the side.

Tiered fountain

DisplayWater
Footprint5×5 round
Key materialsStone, whitestone
Best forA centerpiece fed by a header tank

Stacked stone basins overflow from one to the next. Needs a pressure source uphill to keep it running.

Aqueduct span

Carry waterArches
Footprint12 span
Key materialsStone
Best forCarrying water level across a dip

A line of stone arches holds a channel at a gentle grade. Keep the slope tiny so the water keeps flowing.

Bathhouse

WashHot water
Footprint7×6
Key materialsStone, plaster, a furnace
Best forHeated pools fed by a boiler

A walled hall over a hot pool with a stoking furnace at the back. A chimney carries the smoke clear.

Trough & pump

WaterAnimals
Footprint5×3
Key materialsStone trough, a hand pump
Best forWatering stock from a pump

A long stone trough kept topped by a hand pump. Set it on the paddock fence line so beasts can reach it.

Rain-barrel stand

Catch rainSimple
Footprint4×3
Key materialsTimber, barrels
Best forCatching roof runoff into barrels

Barrels on a low stand under a sloped catch board. The cheapest way to bank rainwater.

Sluice & weir

ControlGate
Footprint8 wide
Key materialsStone sill, a timber gate
Best forHolding back and releasing a stream

A stone weir with a liftable timber gate to set the water level. Raise it to flush, drop it to pond up.

Fountain plaza

PublicDisplay
Footprint9×9 paved
Key materialsPaving, stone, jets
Best forA grand public water feature

A paved square around a big basin with jets and corner posts. The social heart of a town square.

Canal lock

NavigationGates
Footprint12×5 chamber
Key materialsStone walls, mitre gates
Best forStepping boats up a level

A walled chamber with mitre gates at each end fills or empties to raise a boat between two canal reaches.

Ship lift

LiftCaisson
Footprint7×6 × tall
Key materialsTimber tower, a water caisson
Best forHoisting a boat in a water box

A boat floats into a water-filled caisson that is raised whole between canal levels. Counterweights take the load.

Beam pump house

PumpingEngine
Footprint6×5 × tall
Key materialsStone house, a rocking beam
Best forLifting mine water with a beam pump

A tall stone engine house carries a great rocking beam through its end wall to drive a deep pump rod.

Water clock

TimekeepingDisplay
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone plinth, basins, a float dial
Best forA public clepsydra timekeeper

Water drips at a steady rate from basin to basin; a rising float turns a dial. A clock that needs no gearing weights.

Public baths

WashComplex
Footprint11×8
Key materialsStone halls, a domed hot room, a furnace
Best forA staged hot, warm and cold bath suite

A run of halls steps from a domed hot room down to open cold pools, all heated from a furnace at one end.

Reservoir dam

StorageSpillway
Footprint13 wide
Key materialsStone dam, a reservoir, a spillway
Best forBanking a hillside reservoir

A curved stone dam holds back a deep reservoir; a stepped spillway sheds the overflow safely downstream.

Treadwheel well-house

LiftingManual
Footprint5×5
Key materialsStone house, a great tread wheel
Best forRaising deep well water by foot

A person walks inside a big wheel to wind the rope from a very deep well, all under a small tiled house.

Drinking fountain

PublicSpout
Footprint5×5 plaza
Key materialsWhitestone, a spout column, basins
Best forA free public drinking spout

A carved column spouts into a low basin on a small paved island, with bollards to keep carts off. Town water for all.

Washhouse

LaundryOpen
Footprint7×5 open
Key materialsPosts, stone sinks, a roof
Best forA covered communal laundry basin

A long stone basin under an open roof where folk kneel to wash and rinse. Spring water runs in one end and out the other.

Irrigation sluice

ControlPenstock
Footprint8 wide
Key materialsStone channel, a rack-and-pinion gate
Best forMetering water to field channels

A geared penstock gate winds up and down to ration water from a main channel into the field ditches.

10 — Keeping trouble out · 17 builds

Defence & fortification

Walls, gates and strongpoints: palisades, curtain walls, gatehouses, bastions, moats and drawbridges.

Palisade gate

WallLogs
Footprint10 wall + gate
Key materialsLogs
Best forWalling a base against drifters and wolves

A pointed-top log wall broken by a gatehouse. Keeps night creatures out of your compound.

Stone gatehouse

FortifiedPortcullis
Footprint9 wide
Key materialsStone
Best forA defensible main entrance

Twin towers with a battlemented bridge and a portcullis between. The strong point of a curtain wall.

City wall & walk

RampartWalkway
Footprint10 length
Key materialsStone
Best forEnclosing a settlement

A thick wall with a walkway, crenellations and a stair up. Run it between gatehouses and towers.

Signal beacon

LookoutFire
Footprint2×2 pillar
Key materialsStone, an iron basket
Best forRaising the alarm or marking home

A stone pillar with a fire basket on top. Light it to signal across a valley or guide yourself back.

Blockhouse

FortOverhang
Footprint5×5
Key materialsLogs, shingles
Best forA small, stubborn strongpoint

A squat fort with an overhanging upper floor and slit windows. Hard to rush, easy to defend.

Palisade corner & walk

LogsCorner
Footprint9×9 corner
Key materialsLogs, planks
Best forTurning a wall around a corner with a fighting walk

Two pointed log runs meet at a corner, with a plank walkway behind. Stand on the walk to fight over the top.

Curtain wall & drum tower

RampartRound tower
Footprint11 length + tower
Key materialsStone
Best forA round tower anchoring a stretch of curtain wall

A crenellated curtain meets a taller round drum tower. Round towers shed fire and cover the wall faces.

Square mural tower

StoneBattlement
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone
Best forA square tower projecting from a wall line

A tall square tower with machicolated top and arrow slits. Set it astride the wall so it flanks both faces.

Angled bastion

Star fortCannon
Footprint8×7 angled
Key materialsStone, sloped earth
Best forAn arrowhead bastion covering two curtain faces

A pointed earth-and-stone bastion with sloped faces, no dead ground. Mount guns on the flat top platform.

Barbican

GateKilling ground
Footprint9×8
Key materialsStone
Best forA forward gate trap before the main entrance

Two flanking blocks form a narrow walled lane to the gate. Attackers funnel in and get caught from both sides.

Twin-drum gatehouse

GateRound towers
Footprint9 wide
Key materialsStone
Best forA heavily defended main gate flanked by round towers

Two round drums flank a battlemented gate passage. Rounded fronts deflect rams and give wide cover.

Moat & drawbridge

WaterDrawbridge
Footprint10×8 + moat
Key materialsStone, timber, chains
Best forA water moat crossed by a raisable bridge

A stone gate behind a flooded moat, reached by a timber drawbridge. Raise the deck to cut the only crossing.

Ravelin

OutworkTriangular
Footprint8×6 detached
Key materialsSloped earth, stone
Best forA detached triangular outwork before a curtain

A free-standing triangular work that splits an assault before it reaches the main wall. Low, sloped, hard to storm.

Fortified gate & portcullis

GateIron grille
Footprint6 wide
Key materialsStone, iron
Best forA single strong gate with a dropping iron grille

A stone arch with a raised iron portcullis and machicolation above. Drop the grille to seal the passage fast.

Watch post on the wall

RampartBartizan
Footprint10 length + turret
Key materialsStone, shingles
Best forA covered sentry turret cantilevered off a wall

A small roofed bartizan juts from the wall walk so a sentry stays dry and sees along the face below.

Arrow & loophole tower

TowerArrow slits
Footprint3×3 × tall
Key materialsStone
Best forA slim tower bristling with arrow loops

A narrow stone tower pierced with cross-shaped loops on every level. Archers cover a wide arc, exposing little.

Fighting platform

HoardingTimber
Footprint9 length + scaffold
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA timber fighting deck braced against a wall

A bracketed timber hoarding overhangs the wall so defenders drop missiles straight down the face.

11 — Over the gap · 13 builds

Crossings & transport

Span the gap and move goods: bridges, causeways, jetties, roads, rail and carts.

Stone-pier bridge

CrossingStone
Footprint12 span
Key materialsStone piers, timber deck
Best forCrossing a river or ravine dry-shod

Two stone piers carry a timber deck with railings. Sink the piers to the riverbed.

Arched bridge

StoneHumpback
Footprint12 span
Key materialsStone
Best forA grand, all-stone crossing

A humped stone span with an arch beneath. Heavier to build than a deck, but it lasts and looks the part.

Jetty & dock

WaterAccess
Footprint7 reach
Key materialsPosts, planks
Best forReaching deep water or mooring a boat

Planks on posts walking out over the shallows. Stand at the end to fish deeper or tie up a boat.

Boathouse

WaterShelter
Footprint6×4 over water
Key materialsPosts, timber, shingles
Best forKeeping a boat out of the weather

A roofed bay open to the water with a boat berth inside. Build the back on the bank, the front on posts.

Aqueduct

WaterArches
Footprint12 length
Key materialsStone
Best forCarrying water across a dip

Piers carrying a stone channel of running water. Keep a gentle downhill slope so it flows the whole way.

Plank footbridge

CrossingSimple
Footprint9 span
Key materialsPosts, planks, rail
Best forA quick footbridge over a stream

Planks on a couple of posts with a single handrail. The cheapest way across a narrow stream.

Suspension bridge

RopeTowers
Footprint12 span
Key materialsTowers, rope, planks
Best forA long span over a deep gorge

Two towers carry a cabled deck that sags between them. Dramatic, and the only way over a wide gorge.

Stepping stones

FordStones
Footprint10 reach
Key materialsBoulders
Best forHopping a shallow stream dry

A staggered line of flat-topped boulders across the shallows. No build cost beyond hauling rocks.

Raised causeway

RoadStone
Footprint12 length
Key materialsStone embankment
Best forA dry road across marsh or shallows

A solid stone embankment carrying the road above the water. Heavy to build but never washes out.

Log bridge

CrossingLogs
Footprint9 span
Key materialsRound logs
Best forA rough crossing from felled trees

A couple of stout round logs laid across with a few cross-planks on top. Quick, with timber to spare.

Covered bridge

RoofedCrossing
Footprint10 span
Key materialsTimber, shingles
Best forA crossing that keeps the deck dry

A planked deck walled and roofed against the weather. The cover keeps the timbers from rotting.

Ford & markers

CrossingDepth posts
Footprint10 reach
Key materialsGravel, depth posts
Best forDriving a cart through shallow water

A gravelled shallow with painted depth posts to either side. Cross only when the marks read safe.

Cart track & gate

RoadField gate
Footprint11 length
Key materialsGravel, a field gate
Best forA farm track through a gated fence

A rutted gravel track running up to a swing field gate in the fence line. Wide enough for a loaded cart.

12 — Down & deep · 16 builds

Mining & underground

The works around a dig: adits, headframes, shafts, sorting floors and prospect camps.

Mine adit

EntranceTimbered
Footprint7×4 portal
Key materialsSoil, timber, rails
Best forA horizontal way into a hillside

A timbered portal into a bank, with rails and a cart. Frame the mouth so it doesn't cave.

Mine headframe

ShaftHoist
Footprint3×3 × tall
Key materialsTimber, a wheel
Best forLifting ore from a vertical shaft

A timber tower with a hoist wheel over the shaft and a winch house alongside. Raises ore and miners.

Root cellar

Cool storeBuried
Footprint5×4 mound
Key materialsSoil, timber doors
Best forKeeping food cool underground

An earth mound with sloped bulkhead doors over steps down. Stable cool temperatures preserve food.

Ice house

Cold storeDome
Footprint5×5 dome
Key materialsStone, thatch
Best forStoring ice and frozen food

A thick stone dome with a thatch skirt, mostly buried. Packed with winter ice, it stays cold for months.

Grain silo

Bulk storageRound
Footprint4×4 round × tall
Key materialsPlaster/metal, timber
Best forStoring grain in bulk

A tall round bin with a domed cap and a side chute. Fill from the top, draw from the bottom.

Market stall

TradeAwning
Footprint5×3
Key materialsTimber, cloth awning
Best forSelling goods to traders and players

A counter under a striped awning with goods on display. The friendly face of a busy base.

Vertical mineshaft

ShaftWindlass
Footprint4×4 + headgear
Key materialsTimber, a windlass
Best forSinking and working a vertical shaft

A timbered collar over the shaft with a windlass and a small headgear to wind buckets up and down.

Ore-sorting floor

DressingChutes
Footprint7×6
Key materialsTimber tables, chutes
Best forHand-picking and grading raw ore

Picking tables under cover with chutes that drop graded ore into bins below. Sort before you smelt.

Prospect camp

ScoutingTent
Footprint6×5
Key materialsCanvas tent, tools
Best forWorking a new dig before it pays

A canvas tent, a tool rack and a tray of ore samples. The first foothold on a fresh seam.

Spoil heap & tipper

WasteTip cart
Footprint8×5 + rails
Key materialsSoil, rails, a cart
Best forDumping waste rock off a tip

A rising bank of spoil with a rail track and a tipping cart at the lip. Run the dead rock out of the way.

Quarry working face

StoneBenches & crane
Footprint9×7
Key materialsStepped stone, a crane
Best forCutting stone from a stepped face

A worked face cut in stepped benches with a jib crane lifting blocks. Take it down one ledge at a time.

Gravel pit & sluice

GravelWashing
Footprint8×6 + sluice
Key materialsDug gravel, a sluice
Best forWashing gravel to pan out ore

A dug pit with a sloped sluice running water over gravel. Pebbles wash away, the heavy stuff stays.

Mine office shack

OfficeRecords
Footprint5×4
Key materialsPlanks, shingles
Best forTracking ore, pay and shifts

A plank shack with a stovepipe and a notice board by the door. The dry desk in a muddy works.

Powder & tool store

BlastingLocked
Footprint4×4
Key materialsStone, an earth berm
Best forStoring powder and gear safely

A squat stone store banked with earth on the blast side. Keep the dangerous stock well clear of the dig.

Ventilation shaft

AirFan house
Footprint5×5
Key materialsStone, timber, a fan
Best forDrawing fresh air down a deep mine

A capped shaft beside a fan house whose wheel pulls foul air up and out. Deep workings need a draught.

Ore stockpile bins

StockpileHoppers
Footprint8×4
Key materialsTimber bins, ore
Best forHolding graded ore before smelting

A run of timber hopper bins, each filled with a different ore. Draw from the bottom as the furnace needs.

13 — Faith & memory · 12 builds

Temples & monuments

Build for awe: shrines, chapels, cathedrals, standing stones, obelisks, statues and tombs.

Wayside shrine

FaithRoadside
Footprint2×2
Key materialsStone, timber, a candle
Best forA small offering by the road

A stone plinth with a gabled niche and a lit candle. The simplest mark of faith on a journey.

Small chapel

FaithCross
Footprint5×7
Key materialsWhitestone, shingles
Best forA village place of worship

A single nave under a steep gable, topped with a cross. Round-arched door, lancet windows.

Church with steeple

FaithSteeple
Footprint6×9 + tower
Key materialsStone, slate
Best forA parish church and landmark

A long nave with a square bell tower and a tall spire over the entrance. Light the spire as a beacon.

Cathedral facade

GrandTwin towers
Footprint9×4 facade
Key materialsWhitestone, slate
Best forAn awe-inspiring west front

Twin towers flanking a rose window over a great door. Build the nave deep behind it.

Standing-stone circle

AncientRing
Footprint9×9 ring
Key materialsRough menhirs
Best forA ritual ring on open ground

A ring of rough uprights with a few lintels. Set them slightly uneven for an ancient feel.

Single menhir

AncientMarker
Footprint2×2
Key materialsOne great stone
Best forA lone marker on the moor

A single leaning monolith on a low cairn base. Crude, tall, and unmistakable from afar.

Obelisk

MonumentTall
Footprint2×2 × tall
Key materialsDressed stone
Best forA commemorative spike

A tapered four-sided shaft with a pyramidion cap on a stepped base. Gild the tip if you can.

Statue on a plinth

MonumentFigure
Footprint3×3
Key materialsStone, bronze
Best forHonouring a founder or hero

A robed figure on a tall plinth, with a plaque face. Stylise the body from stacked blocks and a dome head.

Mausoleum

TombDome
Footprint5×5
Key materialsWhitestone, copper
Best forA grand resting place

A square stone tomb with a columned front and a copper dome. Solemn, symmetrical, sealed.

Stepped ziggurat

AncientTiered
Footprint9×9 stepped
Key materialsMud brick, stone
Best forA massive tiered temple-mount

Three receding terraces with a central stair to a shrine on top. Build it big and blocky.

Dolmen

AncientCapstone
Footprint4×4
Key materialsThree great stones
Best forA primitive tomb portal

Two uprights and a sloped capstone. The oldest kind of monument; rough and heavy.

Memorial cairn

MarkerStacked
Footprint3×3 mound
Key materialsLoose stones
Best forA heaped stone memorial

A conical heap of stones topped with a small marker. Add one stone each time you pass.

14 — Town life · 12 builds

Civic & social

The shared buildings of a settlement: markets, inns, halls, libraries, schools and bathhouses.

Market stalls

TradeOpen
Footprint9×6 cluster
Key materialsPosts, striped awnings
Best forA lively open-air market

A cluster of awning stalls around an open square. Vary the awning colours for a busy look.

Covered market hall

TradeHall
Footprint9×6
Key materialsTimber posts, tile roof
Best forA sheltered trading hall

An open-sided hall on stout posts under a hip roof. Stalls run down the middle, traders all weather.

Inn & tavern

LodgingSign
Footprint7×6 × 2
Key materialsTimber frame, thatch
Best forFood, drink and a bed for travellers

A two-storey timber inn with a hanging sign and a big chimney. Jettied upper floor for character.

Town hall

CivicClock tower
Footprint8×6 + tower
Key materialsStone, slate
Best forThe seat of town government

A dignified stone hall with a central clock tower and a cupola. Symmetry signals authority.

Guildhall

CivicTrade body
Footprint8×6 × 2
Key materialsTimber frame, tile
Best forA wealthy trade guild's seat

A broad timber-frame hall with a decorated upper storey and an oriel window. Display the guild colours.

Library

CivicLearning
Footprint8×6
Key materialsStone, copper roof
Best forKeeping books and records

A calm stone hall with tall windows for reading light and a columned porch. Cool, quiet, fireproof.

Schoolhouse

CivicBell
Footprint6×5
Key materialsPlaster, shingles
Best forA one-room village school

A modest hall with a little bell cote over the door. One big room, lots of windows.

Courthouse

CivicPortico
Footprint8×6
Key materialsWhitestone
Best forA temple-front hall of justice

A classical portico of columns under a triangular pediment. Grave and imposing.

Public bathhouse

CivicDome
Footprint7×6 + pool
Key materialsStone, copper, water
Best forCommunal hot baths

A domed stone hall with an open plunge pool in front. Steam, warmth and gossip.

Well plaza

CivicGathering
Footprint8×8 square
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA village gathering point

A covered well at the centre of a paved square ringed with benches and a tree. The social heart.

Notice-board square

CivicSigns
Footprint6×5
Key materialsTimber, paper
Best forPosting town news and laws

A roofed notice board with pinned bills, a couple of benches and a lamp. Where news spreads.

Almshouse row

CivicCharity
Footprint12×5 row
Key materialsBrick, tile
Best forLodging for the poor and old

A long row of small identical dwellings under one roof, each with its own door. Charitable housing.

15 — Pure cosmetic · 12 builds

Decorative & garden

Nothing functional, all charm: gazebos, pergolas, fountains, hedges, topiary, ponds, benches and lamps.

Gazebo

CosmeticHex roof
Footprint5×5 round
Key materialsTimber, shingles
Best forA shaded seat in the garden

An open hexagonal pavilion on slim posts with a conical roof and a finial. Pure ornament.

Pergola walk

CosmeticTrellis
Footprint9×3 walk
Key materialsTimber, vines
Best forA shaded vine-clad walkway

A run of paired posts with cross beams and trailing greenery. Walk through dappled shade.

Tiered fountain

CosmeticWater
Footprint5×5 round
Key materialsStone, water
Best forA centrepiece for a plaza

A round basin with stacked tiers and a jet on top. The classic plaza ornament.

Hedge maze

CosmeticHedges
Footprint9×9
Key materialsClipped hedges
Best forA puzzle garden to wander

Squared blocks of clipped hedge laid out as a small labyrinth. Keep the paths one block wide.

Topiary set

CosmeticClipped
Footprint7×4
Key materialsShaped shrubs
Best forSculpted greenery for a formal garden

Shrubs clipped into balls, cones and spirals in matching planters. A tidy formal touch.

Flower-bed parterre

CosmeticBeds
Footprint8×8
Key materialsLow hedge, flowers
Best forA formal patterned flower garden

Four flower beds edged in low hedge around a central urn, paths between. Best seen from above.

Garden pond & bridge

CosmeticWater
Footprint8×6 pond
Key materialsWater, stone, timber
Best forA tranquil ornamental pond

An irregular pond with reeds and an arched timber bridge across one end. Calm and pretty.

Arbor seat

CosmeticVines
Footprint3×3
Key materialsTimber, roses
Best forA vine-covered seat nook

A curved trellis arbor sheltering a bench, smothered in climbing roses. A private corner.

Sundial

CosmeticStone
Footprint2×2
Key materialsStone, bronze
Best forA decorative garden timepiece

A bronze gnomon on a round dial atop a baluster pedestal. Set it on a low plinth in the open.

Bench & lamp post

CosmeticLit
Footprint4×3
Key materialsTimber, iron
Best forA lit place to sit a while

A slatted bench beside an iron lamp post on a paved patch. Simple, charming, useful at night.

Rock garden

CosmeticZen
Footprint6×5
Key materialsBoulders, gravel, moss
Best forA low-maintenance stone garden

Set boulders amid raked gravel with cushions of moss and a lantern. Quiet and meditative.

Raised planter terrace

CosmeticTiered
Footprint7×5 tiers
Key materialsStone, soil, plants
Best forStepped beds on a slope

Three retained terraces of planting climbing a low slope, with stairs up the side. Greens a bank.

16 — Going up · 12 builds

Towers & verticals

Tall things for sight, sound and show: watchtowers, clock and bell towers, observatories and spires.

Tall stone watchtower

LookoutLandmark
Footprint4×4 × very tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA high stone lookout with a covered top

A tapered stone shaft under a roofed watch room with overhang. Height buys sightlines; the roof keeps the watch dry.

Clock tower

CivicClock face
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA town clock visible across the square

A square stone tower with a clock face decal near the top under a steep cap. Site it where the whole town sees it.

Bell tower

BelfryOpen top
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forAn open belfry hung with a bell

A stone shaft topped by an open arched belfry housing a bell. Open sides let the sound carry across the valley.

Observatory

DomeAstronomy
Footprint5×5 round + dome
Key materialsStone, copper
Best forA round tower under a rotating dome

A round stone drum capped by a copper dome with a viewing slot. Build it dark and high, away from torch glow.

Timber siege tower

SiegeWheels
Footprint5×5 × tall
Key materialsTimber, hides
Best forA mobile assault tower on wheels

A wheeled timber tower clad in hides with a drop ramp at the top. Roll it to a wall and storm across the bridge.

Slender spire

SteepleLandmark
Footprint3×3 × very tall
Key materialsStone, slate
Best forA tall thin steeple as a landmark

A narrow stone base bursting into a tall slate spire. Pure vertical drama; tops it with a finial.

Semaphore tower

SignalRelay
Footprint3×3 × tall
Key materialsTimber, planks
Best forA signal relay with movable arms

A timber tower carrying a mast with pivoting signal arms. Chain several on hilltops to pass word fast.

Round beacon tower

BeaconBrazier
Footprint4×4 round × tall
Key materialsStone, iron
Best forA round tower carrying a fire brazier

A round stone tower topped by a railed iron brazier. Keep it stocked with fuel to fire at the first alarm.

Pigeon-post tower

MessengerDovecote
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsPlaster, timber
Best forA messenger-pigeon loft for fast word

A tall plastered loft full of nest holes with a landing shelf. Carrier birds fly word home faster than any rider.

Gate-flanking turret

TurretPepperpot
Footprint3×3 round × tall
Key materialsStone, slate
Best forA slim round turret beside a gate

A pepperpot turret with a conical cap, paired beside a gate to cover the approach. Slim, tall and watchful.

Standard tower

BannerLookout
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone, cloth
Best forA battlemented tower flying a banner

A square battlemented tower with a tall mast and banner. Flies your colours over the keep for all to see.

Stair tower

AccessExternal stair
Footprint4×4 + stair
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA tower with an external climbing stair

A round stair wraps a square tower to reach the top without eating interior space. Cap it with a small watch room.

17 — By the sea · 12 builds

Coastal & naval

Where land meets water: lighthouses, piers, boathouses, shipyards, fish racks and harbour cranes.

Tall lighthouse

BeaconStriped
Footprint5×5 round × very tall
Key materialsWhitewashed stone, glass
Best forA towering striped sea light

A very tall banded round tower under a glazed lantern. Build it on the outermost point you can defend.

Stone harbour pier

QuayBollards
Footprint10×4 + water
Key materialsStone, iron
Best forA solid stone quay with mooring bollards

A heavy stone pier walking out into deep water, lined with iron bollards. Tie large vessels alongside to load.

Long timber jetty

PilingsWalkway
Footprint12 reach
Key materialsPosts, planks
Best forA long piled walk out to deep mooring

Plank decking on tall pilings reaching far over shallows to deep water. Moor a boat or fish off the end.

Boathouse over water

BerthRoofed
Footprint6×5 over water
Key materialsPosts, timber, shingles
Best forA roofed berth open to the sea

A gabled shed standing on pilings with an open water bay for a boat. Back wall on the bank, front on posts.

Ship slipway

ShipyardHull
Footprint10×6 ramp
Key materialsTimber, ribs
Best forA sloped slip with a hull under construction

A timber ramp running into the water bearing a half-planked hull on its keel blocks. Launch by sliding it down.

Fish-drying racks

FishCuring
Footprint8×5
Key materialsPoles, line
Best forOpen rows of racks for drying the catch

Rows of pole frames hung with split fish to wind-dry. Site them in the breeze above the high-tide line.

Dockside crane

CargoTreadwheel
Footprint5×5 + jib
Key materialsTimber, rope
Best forA treadwheel crane lifting cargo onto the quay

A timber treadwheel crane with a swinging jib over the quay edge. Walk the wheel to hoist barrels from a hold.

Breakwater mole

StoneHarbour
Footprint12 length
Key materialsStone, rubble
Best forA rubble mole sheltering the anchorage

A long sloped stone mole takes the swell so the water behind stays calm. Top it with a walkway and a small light.

Raised fishing platform

StiltsNet
Footprint5×5 over water
Key materialsPosts, planks
Best forA railed deck on stilts with a lift net

A railed plank deck on tall posts with a hut and a dip net on a boom. Haul the net to scoop a catch.

Harbourmaster's house

CustomsOffice
Footprint6×5 + steps
Key materialsPlaster, timber, slate
Best forA customs office overlooking the quay

A neat two-storey office on the quay edge with a lookout window over the water. Collects dues and watches arrivals.

Careening wharf

RepairHull
Footprint8×6
Key materialsStone, timber
Best forA wharf for heeling a hull over to clean it

A low stone wharf with capstans where a hull is heeled over for scraping and tarring. Work it at low water.

Shipwright's shed

ShipyardOpen shed
Footprint8×6 open
Key materialsPosts, timber, shingles
Best forAn open shed for framing boats by the water

A tall open-sided shed where a boat is framed up under cover beside the slip. Stack timber along the back wall.

18 — Keeping it · 10 builds

Storage & logistics

Hold the surplus: warehouses, silos, granaries, woodsheds, ice houses and barrel yards.

Timber warehouse

BulkLoading hoist
Footprint9×6 + hoist
Key materialsTimber, planks, shingles
Best forStoring sawn lumber under one roof

A big gabled store with a tall loading door and a hoist beam over it for lifting bundles up top.

Woodshed

FirewoodOpen store
Footprint6×4
Key materialsPosts, planks, shingles
Best forKeeping split logs dry and aired

An open-fronted lean-to packed with stacked split logs. Off the ground and under cover to season.

Cooperage store

BarrelsCasks
Footprint6×5
Key materialsPlaster, timber, barrels
Best forStoring and stacking finished casks

A barrel store with casks racked outside and a workshop within. Keep them shaded so staves don't shrink.

Dutch hay barn

HayOpen sides
Footprint8×6
Key materialsPosts, a high roof, hay
Best forStoring loose hay under a roof

A roof on tall posts with no walls, piled high with hay. Air moves through so the crop won't rot.

Grain silo cluster

Bulk grainCylinders
Footprint8×5
Key materialsPlaster/metal, timber
Best forStoring grain across several bins

Three round bins side by side, each domed and fed from the top. Spread the harvest to cut spoilage.

Large ice house

Cold storeBuried
Footprint7×6 mound
Key materialsStone, soil, timber
Best forHolding bulk ice through summer

A deep stone pit under an earth mound with a north-facing porch. Big enough to stock a whole estate.

Root clamp

RootsSoil mound
Footprint8×3 mound
Key materialsStraw, soil
Best forOverwintering roots outdoors

A long ridge of roots layered with straw and capped with soil. Cheap winter store; open one end at a time.

Salt store

SaltDry shed
Footprint5×4
Key materialsStone, plaster, slate
Best forKeeping salt bone dry

A small, tight stone shed with a slate roof and raised floor. Salt draws damp, so seal it well.

Open drying shed

DryingSlatted
Footprint7×5
Key materialsPosts, slats, shingles
Best forAir-drying herbs, fish or tobacco

A roof over slatted racks with open sides. Wind passes through and dries whatever hangs from the rails.

Stone cold-cellar

Cool storeEntrance
Footprint5×5 entrance
Key materialsStone, timber doors
Best forA walk-in cellar dug into stone

A stone-arched porch set into an earth bank, with a heavy door over the steps. The mass keeps it steady cold.

19 — Master works · 22 builds

Advanced contraptions

Big, complex, mechanical: powered mill complexes, ore washeries, crane gantries, clockworks and gear halls.

Windmill mill complex

WindComplex
Footprint10×7 + tower
Key materialsStone tower, a gear hall
Best forA full milling operation off one big mill

A tower mill with an attached gear hall and grain store. The cap drives a shaft down into the work hall.

Sawmill complex

WaterLumber
Footprint11×7 + wheel
Key materialsTimber hall, a waterwheel
Best forBulk plank cutting off a waterwheel

An overshot wheel drives a frame saw in the long hall, with a log deck and plank stacks alongside.

Ore washery

WaterSorting
Footprint9×6 over water
Key materialsTimber launders, sluices
Best forWashing crushed ore over flowing water

Crushed ore is washed down stepped sluices over a pond; the heavy metal drops out and waste runs off.

Treadwheel crane

LiftingManual
Footprint6×5 × tall
Key materialsTimber, a great wheel
Best forHoisting heavy stone on a build site

A walker inside the great wheel reels in the rope to lift loads. The classic cathedral-site crane.

Gantry crane

LiftingFrame
Footprint8×5 frame
Key materialsHeavy timber, a trolley
Best forMoving loads along a fixed beam

A trolley and hoist ride a beam between two tall legs. Spans a yard so loads move the length of the gantry.

Drawbridge

GateCounterweight
Footprint8×6 + moat
Key materialsStone gatehouse, chains
Best forA lifting span across a moat

Chains over the gatehouse raise the timber deck against a counterweight. Drop it to cross, raise it to seal.

Clockwork tower

GearsTall
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsStone, a gear face, a bell
Best forA driven clock and bell over a square

A weight-driven gear train turns the face and strikes the bell on the hour. A landmark and a town timekeeper.

Multi-forge bellows

ForgePowered
Footprint9×6
Key materialsStone hearths, a cam shaft
Best forSeveral forges fed by one drive shaft

A row of forges share a powered shaft that works each bellows in turn. A small smithing production line.

Blast-furnace complex

IronHoist
Footprint8×7 + hoist
Key materialsStone stack, a charging hoist
Best forContinuous iron smelting at scale

A tall stone stack charged from a hoist platform, with bellows and a tap house at the base.

Foundry hall

CastingChimneys
Footprint10×7
Key materialsBrick, stone, tall flues
Best forMelting and casting metal in volume

A long brick hall with crucible furnaces and twin chimneys. Cast molds line the floor under the smoke hoods.

Trip-hammer battery

ForgingPowered
Footprint9×5 + wheel
Key materialsTimber, a cam shaft, anvils
Best forA row of hammers off one waterwheel

One drive shaft works several trip hammers in sequence over their anvils. Heavy, fast bulk forging.

Grain-mill complex

WaterMilling
Footprint11×7 + wheel
Key materialsStone mill, a granary, a wheel
Best forMilling and storing grain at scale

A waterwheel mill joined to a tall granary, with sack hoists in the loft. The full grain-to-flour works.

Grand mill complex

WaterTowers
Footprint13×8 + tower
Key materialsStone halls, a tower, a wheel
Best forA walled multi-hall milling estate

A tall watch tower, a long mill hall and a gabled granary share one race and wheel. The estate's whole milling works.

Ore stamp battery

CrushingPowered
Footprint9×6 + wheel
Key materialsTimber frame, a stamp row, a wheel
Best forA wall of stamps pulverising ore

A cammed shaft lifts a long row of heavy iron-shod stamps that drop into ore boxes. The wheel hammers them in turn.

Foundry & hoist

CastingCrane
Footprint10×8 + crane
Key materialsBrick hall, a jib crane, a flue
Best forCasting heavy work served by a crane

A brick casting hall with a slewing jib crane in the yard to swing crucibles and heavy moulds over the floor.

Crane gantry yard

LiftingYard
Footprint12×8 yard
Key materialsTwin timber gantries, a trolley
Best forA timber yard spanned by travelling cranes

Two parallel timber gantries carry a cross-trolley over a stacked yard, so loads travel anywhere in the bay.

Automaton hall

ClockworkDisplay
Footprint8×6 + tower
Key materialsStone hall, a gear face, figures
Best forA clockwork jack show on the hour

A weight train drives a gear face and a parade of striking figures across the gallery. A marvel over the market.

Chute works

WaterConveyor
Footprint11×7 over water
Key materialsTrestles, a bucket chain, chutes
Best forA bucket conveyor lifting over a pond

An endless bucket chain climbs a trestle to a head house, then chutes the load down across the water. Wheel-driven lift.

Forge bellows hall

ForgePowered
Footprint11×7 + wheel
Key materialsStone hall, twin bellows, a wheel
Best forA big smithy on a wheel-blown blast

Two great water-blown bellows feed a hall of hearths through one shaft. Twin flues carry the smoke; the wheel drives all.

Mine drainage works

PumpingWater
Footprint10×7 + wheel
Key materialsHeadframe, a wheel, pump rods
Best forA waterwheel pumping a mine dry

A big waterwheel works a rod-train across the yard to a timber headframe, lifting water up the shaft in stages.

Cascade watermill

WaterStepped
Footprint12×8 stepped
Key materialsStone mills, three wheels, a leat
Best forThree mills stepping down one falling leat

One leat drops through three small mills in series, each wheel taking its turn before the water falls to the next.

Twin treadwheel crane

LiftingManual
Footprint8×6 × tall
Key materialsTimber tower, twin tread wheels, a jib
Best forA heavy site crane worked by two wheels

Two great wheels on one axle double the haul on a tall slewing jib. The big version of the cathedral-site crane.

20 — Out in the wild · 10 builds

Wilderness & utility

Out-camps and odd jobs: hunting blinds, fishing huts, trapper cabins, lookouts, signposts and cairns.

Raised hunting blind

ElevatedLadder
Footprint3×3 raised
Key materialsPosts, planks, a ladder
Best forWatching a game trail from height

A small enclosed box on tall posts with a ladder up. Slit windows watch the trail; you stay hidden.

Tree stand

Around a trunkPlatform
Footprint5×5
Key materialsA tree, planks, rails
Best forA perch built around a living tree

A railed plank platform wrapped around a trunk, reached by pegs. Cheap height with natural cover.

Fishing hut on a bank

WatersideJetty
Footprint4×4 + jetty
Key materialsPlanks, posts, thatch
Best forA tiny base by good water

A one-room hut at the water's edge with a short jetty out over it. Dry your nets on the rail.

Trapper's cabin

TinyPelt rack
Footprint4×4 + rack
Key materialsLogs, stone, hides
Best forA remote one-man out-camp

A tiny log cabin with a stone chimney and a rack of stretched pelts drying outside the door.

Lookout platform

Tall postsRailed deck
Footprint4×4 × tall
Key materialsPosts, planks, rails
Best forA high open watch over the land

Four tall braced posts carry a railed open deck, reached by a long ladder. Sightlines, not shelter.

Signpost

WayfindingArms
Footprint2×2
Key materialsA post, plank arms
Best forMarking a junction or route home

A stout post with pointing plank arms at different heights. Carve distances and aim each arm true.

Trail cairn

Stacked stonesMarker
Footprint2×2
Key materialsStacked stones
Best forMarking a path across bare ground

A pile of stones tapering to a point, big enough to spot from afar. Stack a chain to mark a route.

Forager's drying rack

PreserveOpen frame
Footprint5×3
Key materialsPoles, cord, a tarp
Best forDrying herbs, meat and fish in camp

A pole frame hung with strips and bundles under a small rain cover. Air-dries the day's foraging.

Bird hide

Low screenConcealed
Footprint5×3
Key materialsPlanks, brush, a slit
Best forWatching wildlife unseen at ground level

A low screen wall with a viewing slit and a brush-topped roof. Sit behind it and stay out of sight.

Wilderness shrine

RoadsideSmall
Footprint3×3
Key materialsStone, timber, an offering
Best forA quiet marker at a special spot

A little stone plinth with a gabled timber canopy over a niche. A landmark with a touch of reverence.

The Pattern Book
Part of The Drifter's Almanac, an unofficial fan-made guide to Vintage Story (v1.22.3).
Vintage Story is by Anego Studios. This guide is not affiliated with them.
Companion pages:
· The Architect (decorative technique)
· The Homestead (course-by-course plans)

The illustrations are stylised isometric massing studies meant to show shape and proportion, not exact block counts or game textures. Sizes, materials and recipes are config-/version-dependent. Your in-game Handbook and creative palette are the final word.
